title: Where Did All the Computer-Science Professors Go?
excerpt: AI companies are stripping universities of their best researchers.
content_html:
Anthropic has poached such an array of high-profile professors that it has become a punch line in academia. “‘I’m joining Anthropic’ is the new meme right now,” Subbarao Kambhampati, a computer-science professor at Arizona State University (who has not joined Anthropic), told us. This month, the AI company hired the chair of UC Berkeley’s department of electrical engineering and computer science, presumably to help build more capable bots. Perhaps more surprisingly, Anthropic has in recent weeks also picked up a Stanford economist, a theoretical physicist from the University of Maryland, and an analytic philosopher from UT Austin.

AI companies are turning into something like mini-universities in their own right. OpenAI employs top mathematicians and physicists—including one who studies black holes, and another who specializes in string theory. At least three computer-science professors joined Meta’s AI lab in late June. DeepMind, like Anthropic, is home to a crew of philosophers. And Anthropic’s recent job postings indicate an interest in hiring legal scholars and political scientists. It’s unclear exactly how many current and former professors are working at AI companies. Across these four firms, we found more than 80—the majority of whom are computer scientists. Some have left academia entirely; others are still working part-time at a university. That number is likely a significant undercount, because we don’t have access to internal data; it also doesn’t include the many professors who have started their own companies, those at other AI start-ups, and those working with the industry in a less formal capacity.

Particularly for AI researchers, these companies have a strong gravitational pull. “Much of the important research is being done in industry now,” Humphrey Shi, a computer-science professor at Georgia Tech who joined Nvidia as a vice president last fall, told us. As he sees it, “If you want to do something that really, truly matters, you probably want to join one of those entities.” Tech firms are making offers—including very enticing salaries—that are hard for academics to refuse. In the process, research that previously would have happened in the open is getting locked up behind closed doors.

For decades, universities were the center of AI research. The field itself officially began at a gathering of researchers at Dartmouth in 1956, and federal funding provided much of the field’s early support. In the early 2010s, Silicon Valley executives began to take AI’s commercial potential more seriously, and set out to hire the best researchers. In 2013, Google paid $44 million to acquire a start-up run by a trio of AI researchers from the University of Toronto. Facebook then hired Yann LeCun, an NYU professor, to establish the company’s AI-research lab; Uber poached some 40 Carnegie Mellon researchers to work on driverless cars. But for the most part, even as more work was being done inside of private companies, many of the newly hired academics at tech companies retained their professorships and established a culture of open research. “Researchers will be strongly encouraged to publish their work,” OpenAI wrote in its founding announcement. (Note the organization’s name.) This norm helped lead to the current AI boom: In 2017, scientists at Google published a research paper that was immediately of interest to OpenAI. Google’s innovation, called the “transformer,” is what the T in ChatGPT stands for.

As AI has taken center stage, Silicon Valley has intensified its efforts to recruit star researchers—and looked beyond computer-science departments. Philosophers help train tech companies’ bots to better interact with humans, and economists study the labor-market implications of AI. Compensation is only part of the draw. The current era of AI research requires massive amounts of computing power. Universities have only a fraction of the resources that Silicon Valley can provide, and the chasm has widened as the Trump administration has cut back on scientific funding. Anca Dragan, a UC Berkeley computer scientist who heads DeepMind’s AI-safety-and-alignment department, wrote to us that she was partly motivated to take the job to acquire “the data, compute, and budget access to make progress on safety at the frontier.” Some professors who remain in academia are also forming partnerships with frontier labs or starting their own AI companies, in part so that they can pursue their research without resource constraints. And in some cases, would-be star Ph.D. students are dropping out of their graduate programs—or skipping them altogether—to pursue careers in AI instead.

Read: Someone finally wants to hire philosophers

Although plenty of research is still happening within universities, many professors told us, the result is a flywheel: As more academics get sucked up by industry, the center of AI research moves further from academia, thus increasing the incentive for remaining researchers to leave. The exodus could have some advantages. Many of the professors currently at the labs are on temporary leave; some will likely return to academia full-time. When they do so, they’ll bring new knowledge about frontier research to their institutions. (Dragan is currently preparing to teach a small Ph.D. seminar on AI safety.) Scientists at AI labs might also innovate at a faster pace. After all, they don’t have to worry about applying for grants or waiting years for an article to go through peer review. “If some of the best research is being done in these companies, then I kind of want colleagues and faculty members to be there,” Chris Gregg, a Stanford computer scientist, told us. “You go where the best research is being done, and if that happens to be at a company, so be it.”

But when academics decamp to AI labs or spend more of their time working on their own companies, universities are left with fewer professors to teach the next generation of researchers. (Gregg said that students occasionally ask why courses are no longer offered. The answer is sometimes that the professor is on leave at an AI company.) When star professors leave universities, it becomes harder for students to do work that helps them stand out to prospective employers, Shi said. To differentiate themselves from other applicants, it helps to have worked with mentors who are doing consequential or cutting-edge research. Jennifer Chayes, the dean of the College of Computing, Data Science, and Society at UC Berkeley, told us she fears that as research concentrates in labs with proprietary models, it will be harder for people outside the labs to use AI models to advance science. “Computer-science departments at universities will survive this,” she said. “I don’t know if our innovation economy will.”

As the AI race intensifies, companies now decline to release much of their research for fear of giving away their competitive advantage. Both Gregg and Chayes said they hear from researchers who have gone to AI labs that they can’t publish the work they want to. “The top few AI companies share some things that are at the cutting edge, but it’s a very narrow slice of the potential AI literature that could exist,” Nathan Lambert, an independent AI researcher, told us. According to one study, when AI experts permanently transition from universities to companies, they publish roughly 65 percent fewer papers a year. Papers that do get published tend to emphasize progress on safety efforts and not frontier AI capabilities; corporate research can help fuel marketing hype. And with competition among AI companies escalating, it’s not only the research that is getting locked down, but AI models too. Last month, when Anthropic released a new powerful model called Fable, the company announced that it would invisibly degrade the model’s ability to do certain AI research. The move was positioned as a safety decision, but the resulting outcry from academia was strong. (Anthropic apologized and changed the policy.)

Read: There’s never been a better time to study computer science

Academics inside of the tech companies are more sanguine. Shi, the Georgia Tech professor who works at Nvidia, said the company is committed to open research, which is partly why he took the job. Meanwhile, Dragan, the Berkeley computer scientist at DeepMind, explained that publishing isn’t everything. “I find a much bigger priority than publishing is engaging in policy and regulation,” she wrote to us. We also reached out to spokespeople at OpenAI, Meta, and Anthropic for comment. Anthropic declined to comment; OpenAI and Meta did not respond.

The implications are potentially profound, not just for AI research, but for science more generally. In Silicon Valley, there’s a popular view that leading AI labs will end up as the primary drivers of science. “AGI has the potential to be the ultimate tool for advancing science and medicine,” DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis wrote last week. Hassabis has previously said that an inspiration for his company is Bell Labs, the R&D arm of AT&T, which employed some 1,200 Ph.D.s at its height in the 1960s. Scientists at the lab earned at least 10 Nobel Prizes, and invented the transistor and the modern solar cell. Already, DeepMind has done Nobel Prize–winning research on protein-structure prediction, and the top models have already demonstrated impressive capabilities in mathematics research. On Sunday, a mathematician who works at Anthropic posted that he had used the company’s Fable model during the World Cup final to resolve a nearly 90-year-old mathematical problem.

But if scientific talent and computing power becomes concentrated inside private firms, Silicon Valley could also end up as a gatekeeper of science. Some are raising concerns: A group of academics recently published a declaration warning about “the increasing involvement of technology companies in mathematical research.” If left unchecked, they argued, the incursion of tech companies into research could affect “the scope and depth of mathematical research itself.” Instead of accelerating science, as the leaders of AI companies claim they will, they might end up suffocating it.

sentences.en: Anthropic has poached such an array of high-profile professors that it has become a punch line in academia.
sentences.zh: Anthropic挖走了如此多的知名教授，以至于成为学术界的笑柄。

sentences.en: “‘I’m joining Anthropic’ is the new meme right now,” Subbarao Kambhampati, a computer-science professor at Arizona State University (who has not joined Anthropic), told us.
sentences.zh: “‘我要加入 Anthropic’ 是现在的新模因，”亚利桑那州立大学计算机科学教授 Subbarao Kambhampati（尚未加入 Anthropic）告诉我们。

sentences.en: This month, the AI company hired the chair of UC Berkeley’s department of electrical engineering and computer science, presumably to help build more capable bots.
sentences.zh: 本月，这家人工智能公司聘请了加州大学伯克利分校电气工程和计算机科学系的系主任，大概是为了帮助构建更强大的机器人。

sentences.en: Perhaps more surprisingly, Anthropic has in recent weeks also picked up a Stanford economist, a theoretical physicist from the University of Maryland, and an analytic philosopher from UT Austin.
sentences.zh: 也许更令人惊讶的是，Anthropic 最近几周还聘请了一位斯坦福大学经济学家、一位来自马里兰大学的理论物理学家和一位来自德克萨斯大学奥斯汀分校的分析哲学家。

sentences.zh: 人工智能公司本身正在变成类似迷你大学的东西。

sentences.en: OpenAI employs top mathematicians and physicists—including one who studies black holes, and another who specializes in string theory.
sentences.zh: OpenAI 聘请了顶尖的数学家和物理学家，其中一位研究黑洞，另一位专门研究弦理论。

sentences.en: At least three computer-science professors joined Meta’s AI lab in late June.
sentences.zh: 六月下旬，至少三名计算机科学教授加入了 Meta 的人工智能实验室。

sentences.en: DeepMind, like Anthropic, is home to a crew of philosophers.
sentences.zh: DeepMind 和 Anthropic 一样，都是一群哲学家的家园。

sentences.en: And Anthropic’s recent job postings indicate an interest in hiring legal scholars and political scientists.
sentences.zh: Anthropic 最近发布的招聘信息表明有兴趣聘请法律学者和政治科学家。

sentences.en: It’s unclear exactly how many current and former professors are working at AI companies.
sentences.zh: 目前尚不清楚究竟有多少现任和前任教授在人工智能公司工作。

sentences.en: Across these four firms, we found more than 80—the majority of whom are computer scientists.
sentences.zh: 在这四家公司中，我们发现了 80 多家公司，其中大多数是计算机科学家。

sentences.en: Some have left academia entirely; others are still working part-time at a university.
sentences.zh: 有些人完全离开了学术界；有些人则完全离开了学术界。其他人仍在大学兼职。

sentences.en: That number is likely a significant undercount, because we don’t have access to internal data; it also doesn’t include the many professors who have started their own companies, those at other AI start-ups, and those working with the industry in a less formal capacity.
sentences.zh: 这个数字可能严重低估，因为我们无法访问内部数据；它还不包括许多自己创办公司的教授、其他人工智能初创企业的教授，以及以非正式身份与该行业合作的教授。

sentences.zh: “许多重要的研究现在都在工业界进行，”佐治亚理工学院计算机科学教授汉弗莱·施（Humphrey Shi）告诉我们，他去年秋天加入英伟达担任副总裁。

sentences.en: As he sees it, “If you want to do something that really, truly matters, you probably want to join one of those entities.”
sentences.zh: 在他看来，“如果你想做一些真正重要的事情，你可能想加入其中一个实体。”

sentences.en: Tech firms are making offers—including very enticing salaries—that are hard for academics to refuse.
sentences.zh: 科技公司正在提供学术界难以拒绝的报价，其中包括非常诱人的薪水。

sentences.en: In the process, research that previously would have happened in the open is getting locked up behind closed doors.
sentences.zh: 在此过程中，以前本应公开进行的研究现在被关在门后。

sentences.en: But for the most part, even as more work was being done inside of private companies, many of the newly hired academics at tech companies retained their professorships and established a culture of open research.

sentences.en: (Note the organization’s name.)
sentences.zh: （记下该组织的名称。）

sentences.zh: 谷歌的创新被称为“变压器”，正是 ChatGPT 中 T 的代表。

sentences.zh: 随着人工智能占据中心舞台，硅谷加大了招募明星研究人员的力度，并将目光投向了计算机科学系以外的领域。

sentences.en: Philosophers help train tech companies’ bots to better interact with humans, and economists study the labor-market implications of AI.
sentences.zh: 哲学家帮助训练科技公司的机器人更好地与人类互动，经济学家研究人工智能对劳动力市场的影响。

sentences.en: Compensation is only part of the draw.
sentences.zh: 补偿只是平局的一部分。

sentences.en: The current era of AI research requires massive amounts of computing power.
sentences.zh: 当前的人工智能研究时代需要大量的计算能力。

sentences.en: Universities have only a fraction of the resources that Silicon Valley can provide, and the chasm has widened as the Trump administration has cut back on scientific funding.
sentences.zh: 大学只拥有硅谷所能提供资源的一小部分，而且随着特朗普政府削减科学经费，这一差距进一步扩大。

sentences.en: Anca Dragan, a UC Berkeley computer scientist who heads DeepMind’s AI-safety-and-alignment department, wrote to us that she was partly motivated to take the job to acquire “the data, compute, and budget access to make progress on safety at the frontier.”
sentences.zh: 加州大学伯克利分校计算机科学家、DeepMind 人工智能安全与协调部门负责人 Anca Dragan 写信给我们，她接受这份工作的部分动机是为了获取“数据、计算和预算，以在前沿安全方面取得进展”。

sentences.en: Some professors who remain in academia are also forming partnerships with frontier labs or starting their own AI companies, in part so that they can pursue their research without resource constraints.
sentences.zh: 一些留在学术界的教授也正在与前沿实验室建立合作伙伴关系或创办自己的人工智能公司，部分原因是他们可以在不受资源限制的情况下进行研究。

sentences.en: And in some cases, would-be star Ph.D.
sentences.zh: 在某些情况下，未来的明星博士。

sentences.en: students are dropping out of their graduate programs—or skipping them altogether—to pursue careers in AI instead.
sentences.zh: 学生们正在放弃研究生课程，或者干脆跳过研究生课程，转而从事人工智能领域的职业生涯。

blocks.html_en: Read: Someone finally wants to hire philosophers

sentences.en: Read: Someone finally wants to hire philosophers
sentences.zh: 阅读：终于有人想聘请哲学家了

blocks.html_en: Although plenty of research is still happening within universities, many professors told us, the result is a flywheel: As more academics get sucked up by industry, the center of AI research moves further from academia, thus increasing the incentive for remaining researchers to leave. The exodus could have some advantages. Many of the professors currently at the labs are on temporary leave; some will likely return to academia full-time. When they do so, they’ll bring new knowledge about frontier research to their institutions. (Dragan is currently preparing to teach a small Ph.D. seminar on AI safety.) Scientists at AI labs might also innovate at a faster pace. After all, they don’t have to worry about applying for grants or waiting years for an article to go through peer review. “If some of the best research is being done in these companies, then I kind of want colleagues and faculty members to be there,” Chris Gregg, a Stanford computer scientist, told us. “You go where the best research is being done, and if that happens to be at a company, so be it.”

sentences.en: Although plenty of research is still happening within universities, many professors told us, the result is a flywheel: As more academics get sucked up by industry, the center of AI research moves further from academia, thus increasing the incentive for remaining researchers to leave.
sentences.zh: 许多教授告诉我们，尽管大学内部仍在进行大量研究，但结果却是飞轮：随着越来越多的学者被工业界吸纳，人工智能研究的中心距离学术界越来越远，从而增加了剩余研究人员离开的动力。

sentences.en: The exodus could have some advantages.
sentences.zh: 外流可能有一些好处。

sentences.en: Many of the professors currently at the labs are on temporary leave; some will likely return to academia full-time.
sentences.zh: 目前实验室的许多教授都在暂时休假；有些人可能会全职返回学术界。

sentences.en: When they do so, they’ll bring new knowledge about frontier research to their institutions.
sentences.zh: 当他们这样做时，他们将为他们的机构带来有关前沿研究的新知识。

sentences.en: (Dragan is currently preparing to teach a small Ph.D.
sentences.zh: （德拉甘目前正在准备教授一名小型博士生。

sentences.en: seminar on AI safety.)
sentences.zh: 人工智能安全研讨会。）

sentences.en: Scientists at AI labs might also innovate at a faster pace.
sentences.zh: 人工智能实验室的科学家也可能以更快的速度进行创新。

sentences.en: After all, they don’t have to worry about applying for grants or waiting years for an article to go through peer review.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，他们不必担心申请资助或等待数年的文章通过同行评审。

sentences.en: “If some of the best research is being done in these companies, then I kind of want colleagues and faculty members to be there,” Chris Gregg, a Stanford computer scientist, told us.
sentences.zh: “如果一些最好的研究是在这些公司进行的，那么我希望同事和教职人员也能在那里，”斯坦福大学计算机科学家克里斯·格雷格告诉我们。

sentences.en: “You go where the best research is being done, and if that happens to be at a company, so be it.”
sentences.zh: “你去的地方正在进行最好的研究，如果这恰好是在一家公司，那就这样吧。”

blocks.html_en: But when academics decamp to AI labs or spend more of their time working on their own companies, universities are left with fewer professors to teach the next generation of researchers. (Gregg said that students occasionally ask why courses are no longer offered. The answer is sometimes that the professor is on leave at an AI company.) When star professors leave universities, it becomes harder for students to do work that helps them stand out to prospective employers, Shi said. To differentiate themselves from other applicants, it helps to have worked with mentors who are doing consequential or cutting-edge research. Jennifer Chayes, the dean of the College of Computing, Data Science, and Society at UC Berkeley, told us she fears that as research concentrates in labs with proprietary models, it will be harder for people outside the labs to use AI models to advance science. “Computer-science departments at universities will survive this,” she said. “I don’t know if our innovation economy will.”

sentences.en: But when academics decamp to AI labs or spend more of their time working on their own companies, universities are left with fewer professors to teach the next generation of researchers.
sentences.zh: 但当学者们转向人工智能实验室或花更多时间在自己的公司工作时，大学教授下一代研究人员的教授就会减少。

sentences.en: (Gregg said that students occasionally ask why courses are no longer offered.
sentences.zh: （格雷格说，学生偶尔会问为什么不再提供课程。

sentences.en: The answer is sometimes that the professor is on leave at an AI company.)
sentences.zh: 答案是有时教授正在人工智能公司休假。）

sentences.en: When star professors leave universities, it becomes harder for students to do work that helps them stand out to prospective employers, Shi said.
sentences.zh: 施说，当明星教授离开大学时，学生就更难从事有助于他们在未来雇主面前脱颖而出的工作。

sentences.en: To differentiate themselves from other applicants, it helps to have worked with mentors who are doing consequential or cutting-edge research.
sentences.zh: 为了将自己与其他申请者区分开来，与正在进行重要或前沿研究的导师合作会有所帮助。

sentences.en: Jennifer Chayes, the dean of the College of Computing, Data Science, and Society at UC Berkeley, told us she fears that as research concentrates in labs with proprietary models, it will be harder for people outside the labs to use AI models to advance science.
sentences.zh: 加州大学伯克利分校计算、数据科学和社会学院院长 Jennifer Chayes 告诉我们，她担心随着研究集中在拥有专有模型的实验室中，实验室外的人将更难使用人工智能模型来推进科学发展。

sentences.en: “Computer-science departments at universities will survive this,” she said.
sentences.zh: “大学的计算机科学系将渡过难关，”她说。

sentences.en: “I don’t know if our innovation economy will.”
sentences.zh: “我不知道我们的创新经济是否会。”

blocks.html_en: As the AI race intensifies, companies now decline to release much of their research for fear of giving away their competitive advantage. Both Gregg and Chayes said they hear from researchers who have gone to AI labs that they can’t publish the work they want to. “The top few AI companies share some things that are at the cutting edge, but it’s a very narrow slice of the potential AI literature that could exist,” Nathan Lambert, an independent AI researcher, told us. According to one study, when AI experts permanently transition from universities to companies, they publish roughly 65 percent fewer papers a year. Papers that do get published tend to emphasize progress on safety efforts and not frontier AI capabilities; corporate research can help fuel marketing hype. And with competition among AI companies escalating, it’s not only the research that is getting locked down, but AI models too. Last month, when Anthropic released a new powerful model called Fable, the company announced that it would invisibly degrade the model’s ability to do certain AI research. The move was positioned as a safety decision, but the resulting outcry from academia was strong. (Anthropic apologized and changed the policy.)

sentences.en: As the AI race intensifies, companies now decline to release much of their research for fear of giving away their competitive advantage.
sentences.zh: 随着人工智能竞赛的加剧，公司现在拒绝公开他们的大部分研究成果，因为担心放弃他们的竞争优势。

sentences.en: Both Gregg and Chayes said they hear from researchers who have gone to AI labs that they can’t publish the work they want to.
sentences.zh: 格雷格和查耶斯都表示，他们从人工智能实验室的研究人员那里听说，他们无法发表他们想要发表的研究成果。

sentences.en: “The top few AI companies share some things that are at the cutting edge, but it’s a very narrow slice of the potential AI literature that could exist,” Nathan Lambert, an independent AI researcher, told us.
sentences.zh: 独立人工智能研究员内森·兰伯特 (Nathan Lambert) 告诉我们：“顶尖的几家人工智能公司分享了一些处于前沿的东西，但这只是可能存在的潜在人工智能文献的一小部分。”

sentences.en: According to one study, when AI experts permanently transition from universities to companies, they publish roughly 65 percent fewer papers a year.
sentences.zh: 一项研究表明，当人工智能专家永久从大学转到公司时，他们每年发表的论文数量会减少约 65%。

sentences.en: Papers that do get published tend to emphasize progress on safety efforts and not frontier AI capabilities; corporate research can help fuel marketing hype.
sentences.zh: 发表的论文往往强调安全工作的进展，而不是前沿的人工智能能力；企业研究可以帮助推动营销炒作。

sentences.en: And with competition among AI companies escalating, it’s not only the research that is getting locked down, but AI models too.
sentences.zh: 随着人工智能公司之间的竞争不断升级，不仅研究受到限制，人工智能模型也受到限制。

sentences.en: Last month, when Anthropic released a new powerful model called Fable, the company announced that it would invisibly degrade the model’s ability to do certain AI research.
sentences.zh: 上个月，当 Anthropic 发布了一个名为 Fable 的新强大模型时，该公司宣布将无形中降低该模型进行某些人工智能研究的能力。

sentences.en: The move was positioned as a safety decision, but the resulting outcry from academia was strong.
sentences.zh: 此举被定位为安全决定，但引起了学术界的强烈抗议。

sentences.en: (Anthropic apologized and changed the policy.)
sentences.zh: （Anthropic 已道歉并更改了政策。）

blocks.html_en: Read: There’s never been a better time to study computer science

sentences.en: Read: There’s never been a better time to study computer science
sentences.zh: 阅读：现在是学习计算机科学的最佳时机

blocks.html_en: Academics inside of the tech companies are more sanguine. Shi, the Georgia Tech professor who works at Nvidia, said the company is committed to open research, which is partly why he took the job. Meanwhile, Dragan, the Berkeley computer scientist at DeepMind, explained that publishing isn’t everything. “I find a much bigger priority than publishing is engaging in policy and regulation,” she wrote to us. We also reached out to spokespeople at OpenAI, Meta, and Anthropic for comment. Anthropic declined to comment; OpenAI and Meta did not respond.

sentences.en: Academics inside of the tech companies are more sanguine.
sentences.zh: 科技公司内部的学者则更为乐观。

sentences.en: Shi, the Georgia Tech professor who works at Nvidia, said the company is committed to open research, which is partly why he took the job.
sentences.zh: 在英伟达工作的佐治亚理工学院教授施表示，该公司致力于开放研究，这也是他接受这份工作的部分原因。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, Dragan, the Berkeley computer scientist at DeepMind, explained that publishing isn’t everything.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，DeepMind 的伯克利计算机科学家 Dragan 解释说，出版并不是一切。

sentences.en: “I find a much bigger priority than publishing is engaging in policy and regulation,” she wrote to us.
sentences.zh: “我发现比出版更重要的是参与政策和监管，”她在给我们的信中写道。

sentences.en: We also reached out to spokespeople at OpenAI, Meta, and Anthropic for comment.
sentences.zh: 我们还联系了 OpenAI、Meta 和 Anthropic 的发言人征求意见。

sentences.en: Anthropic declined to comment; OpenAI and Meta did not respond.
sentences.zh: Anthropic拒绝置评； OpenAI 和 Meta 均未回应。

blocks.html_en: The implications are potentially profound, not just for AI research, but for science more generally. In Silicon Valley, there’s a popular view that leading AI labs will end up as the primary drivers of science. “AGI has the potential to be the ultimate tool for advancing science and medicine,” DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis wrote last week. Hassabis has previously said that an inspiration for his company is Bell Labs, the R&D arm of AT&T, which employed some 1,200 Ph.D.s at its height in the 1960s. Scientists at the lab earned at least 10 Nobel Prizes, and invented the transistor and the modern solar cell. Already, DeepMind has done Nobel Prize–winning research on protein-structure prediction, and the top models have already demonstrated impressive capabilities in mathematics research. On Sunday, a mathematician who works at Anthropic posted that he had used the company’s Fable model during the World Cup final to resolve a nearly 90-year-old mathematical problem.

sentences.en: The implications are potentially profound, not just for AI research, but for science more generally.
sentences.zh: 其影响可能是深远的，不仅对人工智能研究，而且对更广泛的科学而言。

sentences.en: In Silicon Valley, there’s a popular view that leading AI labs will end up as the primary drivers of science.
sentences.zh: 在硅谷，有一种流行的观点认为，领先的人工智能实验室最终将成为科学的主要驱动力。

sentences.en: “AGI has the potential to be the ultimate tool for advancing science and medicine,” DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis wrote last week.
sentences.zh: DeepMind 首席执行官 Demis Hassabis 上周写道：“AGI 有潜力成为推动科学和医学发展的终极工具。”

sentences.en: Hassabis has previously said that an inspiration for his company is Bell Labs, the R&D arm of AT&T, which employed some 1,200 Ph.D.s at its height in the 1960s.
sentences.zh: 哈萨比斯此前曾表示，他的公司的灵感来自贝尔实验室，这是 AT&T 的研发部门，该实验室在 20 世纪 60 年代鼎盛时期雇佣了大约 1,200 名博士。

sentences.en: Scientists at the lab earned at least 10 Nobel Prizes, and invented the transistor and the modern solar cell.
sentences.zh: 该实验室的科学家至少获得了 10 项诺贝尔奖，并发明了晶体管和现代太阳能电池。

sentences.en: Already, DeepMind has done Nobel Prize–winning research on protein-structure prediction, and the top models have already demonstrated impressive capabilities in mathematics research.
sentences.zh: DeepMind 已经在蛋白质结构预测方面进行了诺贝尔奖获奖研究，并且顶级模型已经在数学研究方面展现了令人印象深刻的能力。

sentences.en: On Sunday, a mathematician who works at Anthropic posted that he had used the company’s Fable model during the World Cup final to resolve a nearly 90-year-old mathematical problem.
sentences.zh: 周日，一位在 Anthropic 工作的数学家发帖称，他在世界杯决赛期间使用该公司的 Fable 模型解决了一个近 90 年历史的数学难题。

blocks.html_en: But if scientific talent and computing power becomes concentrated inside private firms, Silicon Valley could also end up as a gatekeeper of science. Some are raising concerns: A group of academics recently published a declaration warning about “the increasing involvement of technology companies in mathematical research.” If left unchecked, they argued, the incursion of tech companies into research could affect “the scope and depth of mathematical research itself.” Instead of accelerating science, as the leaders of AI companies claim they will, they might end up suffocating it.

sentences.en: But if scientific talent and computing power becomes concentrated inside private firms, Silicon Valley could also end up as a gatekeeper of science.
sentences.zh: 但如果科学人才和计算能力集中在私营公司内部，硅谷最终也可能成为科学的守门人。

sentences.en: Some are raising concerns: A group of academics recently published a declaration warning about “the increasing involvement of technology companies in mathematical research.”
sentences.zh: 一些人提出了担忧：一群学者最近发表了一份声明，警告“科技公司越来越多地参与数学研究”。

sentences.en: If left unchecked, they argued, the incursion of tech companies into research could affect “the scope and depth of mathematical research itself.”
sentences.zh: 他们认为，如果不加以控制，科技公司对研究的入侵可能会影响“数学研究本身的范围和深度”。

sentences.en: Instead of accelerating science, as the leaders of AI companies claim they will, they might end up suffocating it.
sentences.zh: 他们并没有像人工智能公司的领导者声称的那样加速科学发展，最终可能会窒息科学。

content_html_zh:
Anthropic挖走了如此多的知名教授，以至于成为学术界的笑柄。 “‘我要加入 Anthropic’ 是现在的新模因，”亚利桑那州立大学计算机科学教授 Subbarao Kambhampati（尚未加入 Anthropic）告诉我们。 本月，这家人工智能公司聘请了加州大学伯克利分校电气工程和计算机科学系的系主任，大概是为了帮助构建更强大的机器人。 也许更令人惊讶的是，Anthropic 最近几周还聘请了一位斯坦福大学经济学家、一位来自马里兰大学的理论物理学家和一位来自德克萨斯大学奥斯汀分校的分析哲学家。

人工智能公司本身正在变成类似迷你大学的东西。 OpenAI 聘请了顶尖的数学家和物理学家，其中一位研究黑洞，另一位专门研究弦理论。 六月下旬，至少三名计算机科学教授加入了 Meta 的人工智能实验室。 DeepMind 和 Anthropic 一样，都是一群哲学家的家园。 Anthropic 最近发布的招聘信息表明有兴趣聘请法律学者和政治科学家。 目前尚不清楚究竟有多少现任和前任教授在人工智能公司工作。 在这四家公司中，我们发现了 80 多家公司，其中大多数是计算机科学家。 有些人完全离开了学术界；有些人则完全离开了学术界。其他人仍在大学兼职。 这个数字可能严重低估，因为我们无法访问内部数据；它还不包括许多自己创办公司的教授、其他人工智能初创企业的教授，以及以非正式身份与该行业合作的教授。

特别是对于人工智能研究人员来说，这些公司具有强大的吸引力。 “许多重要的研究现在都在工业界进行，”佐治亚理工学院计算机科学教授汉弗莱·施（Humphrey Shi）告诉我们，他去年秋天加入英伟达担任副总裁。 在他看来，“如果你想做一些真正重要的事情，你可能想加入其中一个实体。” 科技公司正在提供学术界难以拒绝的报价，其中包括非常诱人的薪水。 在此过程中，以前本应公开进行的研究现在被关在门后。

几十年来，大学一直是人工智能研究的中心。 该领域本身于 1956 年在达特茅斯的一次研究人员聚会中正式开始，联邦资金为该领域提供了大部分早期支持。 2010 年代初期，硅谷高管开始更加重视人工智能的商业潜力，并开始聘请最优秀的研究人员。 2013年，谷歌斥资4400万美元收购了一家由多伦多大学三名人工智能研究人员运营的初创公司。 Facebook 随后聘请了纽约大学教授 Yann LeCun 来建立该公司的人工智能研究实验室； Uber 挖走了大约 40 名卡内基梅隆大学的研究人员来研究无人驾驶汽车。 但在大多数情况下，尽管私营公司内部正在开展更多工作，但许多科技公司新聘用的学者保留了教授职位，并建立了开放研究的文化。 OpenAI 在其成立公告中写道：“我们将大力鼓励研究人员发表他们的研究成果。” （记下该组织的名称。） 这一规范促成了当前的人工智能热潮：2017 年，谷歌的科学家发表了一篇研究论文，立即引起了 OpenAI 的兴趣。 谷歌的创新被称为“变压器”，正是 ChatGPT 中 T 的代表。

随着人工智能占据中心舞台，硅谷加大了招募明星研究人员的力度，并将目光投向了计算机科学系以外的领域。 哲学家帮助训练科技公司的机器人更好地与人类互动，经济学家研究人工智能对劳动力市场的影响。 补偿只是平局的一部分。 当前的人工智能研究时代需要大量的计算能力。 大学只拥有硅谷所能提供资源的一小部分，而且随着特朗普政府削减科学经费，这一差距进一步扩大。 加州大学伯克利分校计算机科学家、DeepMind 人工智能安全与协调部门负责人 Anca Dragan 写信给我们，她接受这份工作的部分动机是为了获取“数据、计算和预算，以在前沿安全方面取得进展”。 一些留在学术界的教授也正在与前沿实验室建立合作伙伴关系或创办自己的人工智能公司，部分原因是他们可以在不受资源限制的情况下进行研究。 在某些情况下，未来的明星博士。 学生们正在放弃研究生课程，或者干脆跳过研究生课程，转而从事人工智能领域的职业生涯。

阅读：终于有人想聘请哲学家了

许多教授告诉我们，尽管大学内部仍在进行大量研究，但结果却是飞轮：随着越来越多的学者被工业界吸纳，人工智能研究的中心距离学术界越来越远，从而增加了剩余研究人员离开的动力。 外流可能有一些好处。 目前实验室的许多教授都在暂时休假；有些人可能会全职返回学术界。 当他们这样做时，他们将为他们的机构带来有关前沿研究的新知识。 （德拉甘目前正在准备教授一名小型博士生。 人工智能安全研讨会。） 人工智能实验室的科学家也可能以更快的速度进行创新。 毕竟，他们不必担心申请资助或等待数年的文章通过同行评审。 “如果一些最好的研究是在这些公司进行的，那么我希望同事和教职人员也能在那里，”斯坦福大学计算机科学家克里斯·格雷格告诉我们。 “你去的地方正在进行最好的研究，如果这恰好是在一家公司，那就这样吧。”

但当学者们转向人工智能实验室或花更多时间在自己的公司工作时，大学教授下一代研究人员的教授就会减少。 （格雷格说，学生偶尔会问为什么不再提供课程。 答案是有时教授正在人工智能公司休假。） 施说，当明星教授离开大学时，学生就更难从事有助于他们在未来雇主面前脱颖而出的工作。 为了将自己与其他申请者区分开来，与正在进行重要或前沿研究的导师合作会有所帮助。 加州大学伯克利分校计算、数据科学和社会学院院长 Jennifer Chayes 告诉我们，她担心随着研究集中在拥有专有模型的实验室中，实验室外的人将更难使用人工智能模型来推进科学发展。 “大学的计算机科学系将渡过难关，”她说。 “我不知道我们的创新经济是否会。”

随着人工智能竞赛的加剧，公司现在拒绝公开他们的大部分研究成果，因为担心放弃他们的竞争优势。 格雷格和查耶斯都表示，他们从人工智能实验室的研究人员那里听说，他们无法发表他们想要发表的研究成果。 独立人工智能研究员内森·兰伯特 (Nathan Lambert) 告诉我们：“顶尖的几家人工智能公司分享了一些处于前沿的东西，但这只是可能存在的潜在人工智能文献的一小部分。” 一项研究表明，当人工智能专家永久从大学转到公司时，他们每年发表的论文数量会减少约 65%。 发表的论文往往强调安全工作的进展，而不是前沿的人工智能能力；企业研究可以帮助推动营销炒作。 随着人工智能公司之间的竞争不断升级，不仅研究受到限制，人工智能模型也受到限制。 上个月，当 Anthropic 发布了一个名为 Fable 的新强大模型时，该公司宣布将无形中降低该模型进行某些人工智能研究的能力。 此举被定位为安全决定，但引起了学术界的强烈抗议。 （Anthropic 已道歉并更改了政策。）

阅读：现在是学习计算机科学的最佳时机

科技公司内部的学者则更为乐观。 在英伟达工作的佐治亚理工学院教授施表示，该公司致力于开放研究，这也是他接受这份工作的部分原因。 与此同时，DeepMind 的伯克利计算机科学家 Dragan 解释说，出版并不是一切。 “我发现比出版更重要的是参与政策和监管，”她在给我们的信中写道。 我们还联系了 OpenAI、Meta 和 Anthropic 的发言人征求意见。 Anthropic拒绝置评； OpenAI 和 Meta 均未回应。

其影响可能是深远的，不仅对人工智能研究，而且对更广泛的科学而言。 在硅谷，有一种流行的观点认为，领先的人工智能实验室最终将成为科学的主要驱动力。 DeepMind 首席执行官 Demis Hassabis 上周写道：“AGI 有潜力成为推动科学和医学发展的终极工具。” 哈萨比斯此前曾表示，他的公司的灵感来自贝尔实验室，这是 AT&T 的研发部门，该实验室在 20 世纪 60 年代鼎盛时期雇佣了大约 1,200 名博士。 该实验室的科学家至少获得了 10 项诺贝尔奖，并发明了晶体管和现代太阳能电池。 DeepMind 已经在蛋白质结构预测方面进行了诺贝尔奖获奖研究，并且顶级模型已经在数学研究方面展现了令人印象深刻的能力。 周日，一位在 Anthropic 工作的数学家发帖称，他在世界杯决赛期间使用该公司的 Fable 模型解决了一个近 90 年历史的数学难题。

但如果科学人才和计算能力集中在私营公司内部，硅谷最终也可能成为科学的守门人。 一些人提出了担忧：一群学者最近发表了一份声明，警告“科技公司越来越多地参与数学研究”。 他们认为，如果不加以控制，科技公司对研究的入侵可能会影响“数学研究本身的范围和深度”。 他们并没有像人工智能公司的领导者声称的那样加速科学发展，最终可能会窒息科学。
